title: Battletech: Alpha Strike with Aces Co-Op/Solo Rules (Overview)
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aVKjY_uB3tA

Daniel (@dungeondive) returns to BattleTech after a thirty-year gap — his last game was around 1993 — with initial thoughts on Alpha Strike and its Aces set for co-op and solo play, played at a friend's table with the same beautifully painted miniatures from his Battle Masters video. The framing is the simplification: where classic BattleTech meant detailed critical-hit charts, per-weapon heat management, and armour pips beyond counting, Alpha Strike compresses an entire 'Mech onto a single dry-erase card. Movement swaps hexes for inches, attacks skip the to-hit roll and go straight to damage across three range bands, and heat becomes a simple currency you spend to push into overdrive. Crucially, Daniel argues the tactical soul survives — initiative still matters (the winner moves last), facing and positioning still decide battles, and simultaneous combat means even a dying 'Mech gets its final shot. The Aces AI is the sophisticated part: each enemy 'Mech runs on its own card deck under a lance commander, filtering targets step by step. It kept the pair on their toes and was probably winning when time ran out. His honest verdict: great co-op, but too much bookkeeping to want solo — the AI is a two-person job. A campaign session with pilot progression is planned next.

----

Daniel loved commanding every rivet of a 'Mech in 1993 but prefers the one-card version today. Has your tolerance for crunch shifted over the years — and which game marked the turning point?
